input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The exemplar focuses on a laboratory‑scale gantry crane operated in a controlled laboratory environment. The physical twin comprises a small‑scale gantry crane with a linear guide rail, an actuated hoist mechanism, and an embedded controller connected to the digital twin. CAD models and analytical kinematic equations represent the crane’s structure, allowing precise position and velocity tracking. Its compact size, standardized components, and reliance on freely available hardware make it highly suitable for research, teaching, and the evaluation of digital‑twin techniques.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers real‑time monitoring, optimization, continuous validation, and visualization services. Monitoring is achieved by continuously logging sensor streams (position, velocity, actuator commands) and feeding this data back into the twin for historical analysis. Optimization is performed by solving the crane’s kinematic equations to generate trajectory plans that minimize time or energy use, supplementing the physical controller. Continuous validation compares live sensor measurements against the CAD/kinematic model to detect discrepancies and trigger fault‑detection routines. Visualization services render the crane’s pose and simulation results in a 3‑D viewer, allowing researchers, educators and developers to observe motion, review logs, and audit performance. The target users are researchers developing twin algorithms, educators demonstrating twin concepts, and developers integrating twin services into broader cyber‑physical‑system environments.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: GreenhouseDT orchestrates its Digital Twin and physical greenhouse through a modular, micro‑service architecture running inside a single virtual machine. The twin system ingests sensor telemetry delivered by the physical greenhouse via an ActiveMQ message broker. InfluxDB serves as the central time‑series database, storing raw readings and derived inference metrics. Each functional block – sensor aggregation, state inference, rule‑based adaptation, and runtime visualization – is deployed as a Docker container managed by Docker Compose, enabling isolated services that can be independently scaled or replaced. Synchronisation between the physical and virtual realms occurs through the message timestamps embedded in the broker messages; the twin applies a 100 ms latency budget to align data streams, and periodic adaptation cycles execute every 5 seconds based on the most recent aggregated state. The orchestrator ensures graceful shutdown and restart of services, preserving consistency across the twin ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Air Quality Management exemplar integrates with external systems using several industry‑standard protocols and services. Sensor data from a Raspberry‑Pi is transmitted over MQTT with TLS encryption to Azure IoT Hub. The twin is modeled in AutomationML, providing semantic interoperability for building automation and facility management systems. Azure Stream Analytics performs real‑time transformations and feeds the results back into the twin via Azure Functions, which then trigger predictive models and update the twin state. Data persistence occurs in Azure Table Storage, while the twin exposes RESTful APIs for external ERP or CMMS integration. ActiveMQ is also employed within the GreenhouseDT context to broadcast events to any collaborating systems, ensuring loose coupling and extensibility across the enterprise landscape.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: GreenhouseDT uses an InfluxDB time-series database to persist greenhouse sensor streams and an ActiveMQ message broker to shuttle real-time data between the physical greenhouse and the twin's simulation layer. The twin’s computational models include a behavioural-adaptation controller that translates real-time greenhouse states into control actions, and a low-cost greenhouse process model derived from prerecorded data bundled in the provided VM images. Air Quality Management employs an AutomationML-based structural model for the indoor air system, and predictive simulation/prediction engines orchestrated through Microsoft Azure tools. Data ingestion is handled via the Raspberry-Pi sensor bus and Azure‑managed pipelines, tying sensor data to the twin’s models through message-bus or RESTful connectors, yielding a multi-scale Digital Twin architecture.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The Physical Crane Interface, implemented in Python, reads real–time state data from the crane’s controllers (including position, velocity and load metrics) and forwards this information to the Logging Application. The Logging Application continuously stores the received data into a TimescaleDB database. No explicit communication protocol, data format, transmission frequency, or data quality assurance steps are detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Digital Twin’s Control Application, also written in Python, consumes optimal movement trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator Service. The Control Application sends movement commands—typically a sequence of target positions or waypoints—through the Physical Crane Interface to the actual crane controllers. Feedback from the physical crane is streamed back to the Control Application, enabling real–time monitoring. The document does not specify authentication, validation, or conflict‑resolution mechanisms for these control messages.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑service communication is orchestrated via an MQTT broker, which routes signals between the various Digital Twin containers (Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, etc.). The system persists data in a TimescaleDB database and visualises it on a Grafana dashboard. The document does not provide additional details on network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, or security protocols.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The GreenhouseDT and Indoor Air Quality (IAQ) exemplars employ an ad‑hoc development methodology characterized by repeated use of containerised or virtualised deployment artefacts. Source code, configuration scripts and documentation are stored in public GitHub repositories, providing a conventional Git‑based version control workflow. The DT modelling layer in the IAQ case is specified using AutomationML, while the GreenhouseDT logical representation is built directly in code and refers to an InfluxDB time‑series database and an ActiveMQ message broker. During the build and deployment stages, Docker containers or pre‑generated virtual machine images on KVM/VMWare are used to initialise the runtime environment. Quality assurance is performed primarily through runtime visualization dashboards and simulation/prediction modules that can be validated against recorded sensor data; integration tests are executed by running the twin pipeline on the same virtualised infrastructure that hosts the physical twin. Versioning of the twin model and data schemas is managed in Git tags, and evolution of the twin is handled by incremental updates to the open‑source code and Oracle tooling in the Azure cloud pipeline for IAQ.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The IAQ exemplar explicitly offers three runtime capabilities: 1) visualisation of current indoor air quality metrics on a web dashboard, 2) simulation of airflow and contaminant transport using the twin model, and 3) prediction of virus spread under different control scenarios. These insights are generated by analytics pipelines running on Microsoft Azure services (Azure Functions and Azure Machine Learning), which ingest sensor data streamed from IoT devices via an Azure IoT Hub front‑end. The twin’s decision support flow maps projected infection risk scores to actionable recommendations such as ventilation level adjustments, which are exposed to users through the dashboard. Confidence in recommendations is implicitly derived from the predictive accuracy reported by the simulation models, although explicit statistical confidence intervals are not documented.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The indoor air quality digital twin conforms to the AutomationML open‑standard for modeling automation systems, providing a machine‑readable, interoperable twin representation. This standardisation choice facilitates the exchange of twin data with conventional building management systems. The use of InfluxDB and ActiveMQ indicates adherence to the standard protocols of time‑series database systems and the OpenWire protocol stack for messaging, respectively, though explicit industry certification is not reported.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil
block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
